question_text: For any two events A and B, if {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}){/tex} = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} , {tex}\text P(\bar {\text B}){/tex} = {tex}\frac 23{/tex} and P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = {tex}\frac 14{/tex} , then {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\overline{\mathrm{A}}}{\overline{\mathrm{B}}}\right){/tex} equals:

options.explanation: {tex} P(\bar{A})=\frac{1}{2}, P(\bar{B})=\frac{2}{3} \text { and } P(A \cap B)=\frac{1}{4} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow P(A)=\frac{1}{2} ; P(B)=\frac{1}{3} {/tex} We have, {tex}P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B){/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{7}{12} {/tex} {tex} P\left(\frac{\bar{A}}{\bar{B}}\right)=\frac{P(\bar{A} \cap \bar{B})}{P(\bar{B})} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{P(\overline{A \cup B})}{P(\bar{B})} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1-P(A \cup B)}{P(\bar{B})} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1-\frac{7}{12}}{\frac{2}{3}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{5}{8} {/tex}

question_text: Two events E and F are independent. If P(E) = 0.3, P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F)=0.5 then P(E|F) - P(F|E) equals

options.explanation: Here P(E) = 0.3, P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F)=0.5 Let P(F) = x {tex}\therefore P(E \cup F)=P(E)+P(F)-P(E \cap F){/tex} {tex}=P(E)+P(F)-P(E) \cdot P(F){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 0.5 = 0.3 + x - 0.3x {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{0.5-0.3}{0.7}=\frac{2}{7}=P(F){/tex} {tex}\therefore P(E / F)-P(F / E)=\frac{P(E \cap F)}{P(F)}-\frac{P(F \cap E)}{P(E)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{P(E \cap F) \cdot P(E)-P(F \cap E) \cdot P(F)}{P(E) \cdot P(F)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{P(E \cap F)[P(E)-P(F)]}{P(E \cap F)}=P(E)-P(F){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{10}-\frac{2}{7}=\frac{21-20}{70}=\frac{1}{70}{/tex}

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
According to recent research, air turbulence has increased in various regions around the world due to climate change. Turbulence makes flights bumpy and often delays the flights.
Assume that, an airplane observes severe turbulence, moderate turbulence or light turbulence with equal probabilities. Further, the chance of an airplane reaching late to the destination are 55%, 37% and 17% due to severe, moderate and light turbulence respectively.

On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find the probability that an airplane reached its destination late. (2)
  2. If the airplane reached its destination late, find the probability that it was due to moderate turbulence. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: According to recent research, air turbulence has increased in various regions around the world due to climate change. Turbulence makes flights bumpy and often delays the flights. Assume that, an airplane observes severe turbulence, moderate turbulence or light turbulence with equal probabilities. Further, the chance of an airplane reaching late to the destination are 55%, 37% and 17% due to severe, moderate and light turbulence respectively. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions: Find the probability that an airplane reached its destination late. (2) If the airplane reached its destination late, find the probability that it was due to moderate turbulence. (2)
explanation: Let A denote the event of airplane reaching its destination late E 1 = severe turbulence E 2 = moderate turbulence E 3 = light turbulence {tex} {P}({A}) ={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)+P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{55}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{17}{100} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{109}{100}\right)=\frac{109}{300} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}}{\frac{109}{300}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{37}{109} {/tex}
solution: Let A denote the event of airplane reaching its destination late E 1 = severe turbulence E 2 = moderate turbulence E 3 = light turbulence {tex} {P}({A}) ={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)+P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{55}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{17}{100} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{109}{100}\right)=\frac{109}{300} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}}{\frac{109}{300}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{37}{109} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let A denote the event of airplane reaching its destination late
    E1 = severe turbulence
    E2 = moderate turbulence
    E3 = light turbulence
    {tex} {P}({A}) ={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)+P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right) {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{55}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{17}{100} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{109}{100}\right)=\frac{109}{300} {/tex}
  2. {tex} {P}\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{37}{100}}{\frac{109}{300}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{37}{109} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
A building contractor undertakes a job to construct 4 flats on a plot along with parking area. Due to strike the probability of many construction workers not being present for the job is 0.65. The probability that many are not present and still the work gets completed on time is 0.35. The probability that work will be completed on time when all workers are present is 0.80.
Let: E1: represent the event when many workers were not present for the job;
E2: represent the event when all workers were present; and
E: represent completing the construction work on time.

  1. What is the probability that all the workers are present for the job? (1)
  2. What is the probability that construction will be completed on time? (1)
  3. What is the probability that many workers are not present given that the construction work is completed on time? (2)
    OR
    What is the probability that all workers were present given that the construction job was completed on time? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A building contractor undertakes a job to construct 4 flats on a plot along with parking area. Due to strike the probability of many construction workers not being present for the job is 0.65. The probability that many are not present and still the work gets completed on time is 0.35. The probability that work will be completed on time when all workers are present is 0.80. Let: E 1 : represent the event when many workers were not present for the job; E 2 : represent the event when all workers were present; and E: represent completing the construction work on time. What is the probability that all the workers are present for the job? (1) What is the probability that construction will be completed on time? (1) What is the probability that many workers are not present given that the construction work is completed on time? (2) OR What is the probability that all workers were present given that the construction job was completed on time? (2)
explanation: P(E 2 ) = 1 - P(E 1 ) = 1 – 0.65 = 0.35 P(E) = P(E 1 ) {tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_1}\right){/tex} + P(E 2 ) {tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_2}\right){/tex} = 0.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.35 + 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 = 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.45 = 0.51 {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)+\text P\left(\text E_2\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.65 \times 0.35}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.45 OR {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}{E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.35 \times 0.8}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.55
solution: P(E 2 ) = 1 - P(E 1 ) = 1 – 0.65 = 0.35 P(E) = P(E 1 ) {tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_1}\right){/tex} + P(E 2 ) {tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_2}\right){/tex} = 0.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.35 + 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 = 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.45 = 0.51 {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)+\text P\left(\text E_2\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.65 \times 0.35}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.45 OR {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}{E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.35 \times 0.8}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.55
solution_html:
1. P(E2) = 1 - P(E1) = 1 – 0.65 = 0.35

  2. P(E) = P(E1){tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_1}\right){/tex} + P(E2){tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_2}\right){/tex}
    = 0.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.35 + 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8
    = 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.45
    = 0.51

  3. {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)+\text P\left(\text E_2\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.65 \times 0.35}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.45
    OR
    {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}{E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.35 \times 0.8}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.55

Based upon the results of regular medical check-ups in a hospital, it was found that out of 1000 people, 700 were very healthy, 200 maintained average health and 100 had a poor health record.
Let {tex}{A}_{1}{/tex}: People with good health,
{tex}{A}_{2}{/tex}: People with average health,
and {tex}{A}_{3}{/tex}: People with poor health.
During a pandemic, the data expressed that the chances of people contracting the disease from category {tex}{A}_{1}, {~A}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}{A}_{3}{/tex} are {tex}25 \%, 35 \%{/tex} and {tex}50 \%{/tex}, respectively.
Based upon the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. A person was tested randomly. What is the probability that he/she has contracted the disease? (2)
  2. Given that the person has not contracted the disease, what is the probability that the person is from category {tex}{A}_{2}{/tex}? (2)
question_text: Based upon the results of regular medical check-ups in a hospital, it was found that out of 1000 people, 700 were very healthy, 200 maintained average health and 100 had a poor health record. Let {tex}{A}_{1}{/tex} : People with good health, {tex}{A}_{2}{/tex} : People with average health, and {tex}{A}_{3}{/tex} : People with poor health. During a pandemic, the data expressed that the chances of people contracting the disease from category {tex}{A}_{1}, {~A}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}{A}_{3}{/tex} are {tex}25 \%, 35 \%{/tex} and {tex}50 \%{/tex} , respectively. question_html:
Recent studies suggest that roughly 12% of the world population is left handed.

Depending upon the parents, the chances of having a left handed child are as follows:

  1. When both father and mother are left handed:
    Chances of left handed child is 24%.
  2. When father is right handed and mother is left handed:
    Chances of left handed child is 22%.
  3. When father is left handed and mother is right handed:
    Chances of left handed child is 17%.
  4. When both father and mother are right handed:
    Chances of left handed child is 9%.

Assuming that P(A) = P(B) = P(C) = P(D) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} and L denotes the event that child is left handed.

  1. Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text C}\right){/tex}. (1)
  2. Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\bar {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex}. (1)
  3. Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text L}\right){/tex}. (2)
    OR
    Find the probability that a randomly selected child is left handed given that exactly one of the parents is left handed. (2)
question_text: Recent studies suggest that roughly 12% of the world population is left handed. Depending upon the parents, the chances of having a left handed child are as follows: When both father and mother are left handed: Chances of left handed child is 24%. When father is right handed and mother is left handed: Chances of left handed child is 22%. When father is left handed and mother is right handed: Chances of left handed child is 17%. When both father and mother are right handed: Chances of left handed child is 9%. Assuming that P(A) = P(B) = P(C) = P(D) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} and L denotes the event that child is left handed. Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text C}\right){/tex} . (1) Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\bar {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} . (1) Find {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text L}\right){/tex} . (2) OR Find the probability that a randomly selected child is left handed given that exactly one of the parents is left handed. (2)
explanation: {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text C}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac {17}{100}{/tex} {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\bar {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} = 1 - {tex}\text P\left(\frac { {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac {24}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {76}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {19}{25}{/tex} {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text A}{\text L}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{24}{100}}{\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{24}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{22}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{17}{100}+\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{9}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{24}{72}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Probability that a randomly selected child is left-handed given that exactly one of the parents is left-handed. = {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text L}{\text B \cup \text C}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{22}{100}+\frac{17}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{39}{100}{/tex}

  2. {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\bar {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} = 1 - {tex}\text P\left(\frac { {\text L}}{\text A}\right){/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac {24}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {76}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {19}{25}{/tex}

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Akshat and his friend Aditya were playing the snake and ladder game. They had their own dice to play the game. Akshat was having red dice whereas Aditya had black dice. In the beginning, they were using their own dice to play the game. But then they decided to make it faster and started playing with two dice together.

Aditya rolled down both black and red die together.
First die is black and second is red.

  1. Find the conditional probability of obtaining a sum greater than 9, given that the black die resulted in a 5. (1)
  2. Find the conditional probability of obtaining the sum 8, given that the red die resulted in a number less than 4. (1)

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
There are two antiaircraft guns, named as A and B. The probabilities that the shell fired from them hits an airplane are 0.3 and 0.2 respectively. Both of them fired one shell at an airplane at the same time.

  1. What is the probability that the shell fired from exactly one of them hit the plane? (1)
  2. If it is known that the shell fired from exactly one of them hit the plane, then what is the probability that it was fired from B? (1)
  3. What is the probability that the shell was fired from A? (2)
    OR
    How many hypotheses are possible before the trial, with the guns operating independently? Write the conditions of these hypotheses. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: There are two antiaircraft guns, named as A and B. The probabilities that the shell fired from them hits an airplane are 0.3 and 0.2 respectively. Both of them fired one shell at an airplane at the same time. What is the probability that the shell fired from exactly one of them hit the plane? (1) If it is known that the shell fired from exactly one of them hit the plane, then what is the probability that it was fired from B? (1) What is the probability that the shell was fired from A? (2) OR How many hypotheses are possible before the trial, with the guns operating independently? Write the conditions of these hypotheses. (2)
explanation: Let P be the event that the shell fired from A hits the plane and Q be the event that the shell fired from B hits the plane. question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
For an audition of a reality singing competition, interested candidates were asked to apply under one of the two musical genres-folk or classical and under one of the two age categories-below 18 or 18 and above.
The following information is known about the 2000 application received:

  1. 960 of the total applications were the folk genre.
  2. 192 of the folk applications were for the below 18 category.
  3. 104 of the classical applications were for the 18 and above category.

Questions:

  1. What is the probability that an application selected at random is for the 18 and above category provided it is under the classical genre? Show your work. (1)
  2. An application selected at random is found to be under the below 18 category. Find the probability that it is under the folk genre. Show your work. (1)
  3. If P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.8 and P(B|A) = 0.6, then P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) is equal to. (2)
    OR
  4. If A and B are two independent events with P(A) = {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex} and P(B) = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}, then find P(A' {tex}\cap{/tex} B'). (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: For an audition of a reality singing competition, interested candidates were asked to apply under one of the two musical genres-folk or classical and under one of the two age categories-below 18 or 18 and above. The following information is known about the 2000 application received: 960 of the total applications were the folk genre. 192 of the folk applications were for the below 18 category. 104 of the classical applications were for the 18 and above category. Questions: What is the probability that an application selected at random is for the 18 and above category provided it is under the classical genre? Show your work. (1) An application selected at random is found to be under the below 18 category. Find the probability that it is under the folk genre. Show your work. (1) If P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.8 and P(B|A) = 0.6, then P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) is equal to. (2) OR If A and B are two independent events with P(A) = {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex} and P(B) = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex} , then find P(A' {tex}\cap{/tex} B'). (2)
explanation: According to given information, we construct the following table. Given, total applications = 2000 Folk Genre Classical Genre 960 (given) 2000 - 960 = 1040 Below 18 192 (given) 1040 - 104 = 936 18 or Above 18 960 - 192 = 768 104 (given) Let E 1 = Event that application for folk genre E 2 = Event that application for classical genre A = Event that application for below 18 B = Event that application for 18 or above 18 {tex}\therefore {/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1040}{2000}{/tex} and P(B {tex}\cap{/tex} E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{104}{2000}{/tex} Required Probability = {tex}\frac{P\left(B \cap E_2\right)}{P\left(E_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{104}{2000}}{\frac{1040}{200}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} Required probability = {tex}P\left(\frac{\text { folk }}{\text { below } 18}\right){/tex} = {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}{A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_1 \cap A\right)}{P(A)}{/tex} Now, P(E 1 {tex}\cap {/tex} A) = {tex}\frac{192}{2000}{/tex} and P(A) = {tex}\frac{192+936}{2000}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1128}{2000}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Required probability = {tex}\frac{\frac{192}{2000}}{\frac{1128}{2000}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{192}{1128}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8}{47}{/tex} Here, P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.8 and P(B|A) = 0.6 {tex}\because{/tex} P(B|A) = {tex}\frac {P(B\cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(B {tex}\cap{/tex} A) = P(B|A).P(A) = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 = 0.24 {tex}\because{/tex} P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = 0.4 + 0.8 - 0.24 = 1.2 - 0.24 = 0.96 OR Since, A and B are independent events, A' and B' are also independent. Therefore, P(A' {tex}\cap {/tex} B') = P(A') {tex}\cdot {/tex} P(B') = (1 - P(A)(1 - P(B)) = {tex}\left(1-\frac{3}{5}\right){/tex} {tex}\left(1-\frac{4}{9}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{5}\cdot\frac{5}{9} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{9}{/tex}

Given, total applications = 2000 Folk Genre Classical Genre 960 (given) 2000 - 960 = 1040 Below 18 192 (given) 1040 - 104 = 936 18 or Above 18 960 - 192 = 768 104 (given) Let E 1 = Event that application for folk genre E 2 = Event that application for classical genre A = Event that application for below 18 B = Event that application for 18 or above 18 {tex}\therefore {/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1040}{2000}{/tex} and P(B {tex}\cap{/tex} E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{104}{2000}{/tex} Required Probability = {tex}\frac{P\left(B \cap E_2\right)}{P\left(E_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{104}{2000}}{\frac{1040}{200}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} Required probability = {tex}P\left(\frac{\text { folk }}{\text { below } 18}\right){/tex} = {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}{A}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_1 \cap A\right)}{P(A)}{/tex} Now, P(E 1 {tex}\cap {/tex} A) = {tex}\frac{192}{2000}{/tex} and P(A) = {tex}\frac{192+936}{2000}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1128}{2000}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Required probability = {tex}\frac{\frac{192}{2000}}{\frac{1128}{2000}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{192}{1128}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8}{47}{/tex} Here, P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.8 and P(B|A) = 0.6 {tex}\because{/tex} P(B|A) = {tex}\frac {P(B\cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(B {tex}\cap{/tex} A) = P(B|A).P(A) = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 = 0.24 {tex}\because{/tex} P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = 0.4 + 0.8 - 0.24 = 1.2 - 0.24 = 0.96 OR Since, A and B are independent events, A' and B' are also independent. 1. According to given information, we construct the following table.
    Given, total applications = 2000

      Folk Genre Classical Genre
      960 (given) 2000 - 960 = 1040
    Below 18 192 (given) 1040 - 104 = 936
    18 or Above 18 960 - 192 = 768 104 (given)

    Let E1 = Event that application for folk genre
    E2 = Event that application for classical genre
    A = Event that application for below 18
    B = Event that application for 18 or above 18
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} P(E2) = {tex}\frac{1040}{2000}{/tex}
    and P(B {tex}\cap{/tex} E2) = {tex}\frac{104}{2000}{/tex}
    Required Probability = {tex}\frac{P\left(B \cap E_2\right)}{P\left(E_2\right)}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\frac{104}{2000}}{\frac{1040}{200}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex}

  2. Required probability = {tex}P\left(\frac{\text { folk }}{\text { below } 18}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}P\left(\frac{E_1}{A}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_1 \cap A\right)}{P(A)}{/tex}
    Now, P(E1 {tex}\cap {/tex} A) = {tex}\frac{192}{2000}{/tex}
    and P(A) = {tex}\frac{192+936}{2000}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1128}{2000}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Required probability = {tex}\frac{\frac{192}{2000}}{\frac{1128}{2000}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{192}{1128}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8}{47}{/tex}

  3. Here,
    P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.8 and P(B|A) = 0.6
    {tex}\because{/tex} P(B|A) = {tex}\frac {P(B\cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(B {tex}\cap{/tex} A) = P(B|A).P(A)
    = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 = 0.24
    {tex}\because{/tex} P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B)
    = 0.4 + 0.8 - 0.24
    = 1.2 - 0.24 = 0.96

question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: There are different types of Yoga which involve the usage of different poses of Yoga Asanas, Meditation and Pranayam as shown in the figure below: The Venn diagram below represents the probabilities of three different types of Yoga, A, B and C performed by the people of a society. explanation: x + 0.21 = 0.44 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0.23 0.41 + y + 0.44 + 0.11 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 0.04 {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text C}{\text B}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P(\text C \cap \text B)}{\text P(\text B)}{/tex} P(B) = 0.09 + 0.04 + 0.23 = 0.36 {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text C}{\text B}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.23}{0.36}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{23}{36}{/tex} OR P(A or B but not C) = 0.32 + 0.09 + 0.04 = 0.45

1. x + 0.21 = 0.44 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0.23

  2. 0.41 + y + 0.44 + 0.11 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 0.04

    P(B) = 0.09 + 0.04 + 0.23 = 0.36
    {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text C}{\text B}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.23}{0.36}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{23}{36}{/tex}

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Mr. Ajay is taking up subjects of mathematics, physics, and chemistry in the examination. His probabilities of getting a grade A in these subjects are 0.2, 0.3, and 0.5 respectively.

  1. Find the probability that Ajay gets Grade A in all subjects. (1)
  2. Find the probability that he gets Grade A in no subjects. (1)
  3. Find the probability that he gets Grade A in two subjects. (2)
    OR
    Find the probability that he gets Grade A in at least one subject. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Mr. Ajay is taking up subjects of mathematics, physics, and chemistry in the examination. His probabilities of getting a grade A in these subjects are 0.2, 0.3, and 0.5 respectively. Find the probability that Ajay gets Grade A in all subjects. (1) Find the probability that he gets Grade A in no subjects. (1) Find the probability that he gets Grade A in two subjects. (2) OR Find the probability that he gets Grade A in at least one subject. (2)

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
There are two antiaircraft guns, named as A and B. The probabilities that the shell fired from them hits an airplane are 0.3 and 0.2 respectively. Both of them fired one shell at an airplane at the same time.

  1. How is Bayes' theorem different from conditional probability? (1)
  2. Write the rule of Total Probability. (1)
  3. What is the probability that the shell fired from exactly one of them hit the plane? (2)
    OR
    If it is know that the shell fired from exactly one of them hit the plane, then what is the probability that it was fired from B? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: There are two antiaircraft guns, named as A and B. The probabilities that the shell fired from them hits an airplane are 0.3 and 0.2 respectively. Both of them fired one shell at an airplane at the same time. How is Bayes' theorem different from conditional probability? (1) Write the rule of Total Probability. (1) What is the probability that the shell fired from exactly one of them hit the plane? (2) OR If it is know that the shell fired from exactly one of them hit the plane, then what is the probability that it was fired from B? (2)

Consider on event E which occurs via two different events A and B. The probability of E is given by the value of total probability as: P(E) = P(A {tex}\cap {/tex} E) + P(B {tex}\cap {/tex} E) P(E) = P(A) P {tex}\left(\frac{E}{A}\right){/tex} + P(B) {tex}\left(\frac{E}{B}\right){/tex} Let P be the event that the shell fired from A hits the plane and Q be the event that the shell fired from B hits the plane. P(E 1 ) = 0.3 {tex}\times {/tex} 0.2 = 0.06, P(E 2 ) = 0.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 = 0.56, P(E 3 ) = 0.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 = 0.14, P(E 4 ) = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 = 0.24 P {tex}\left(\frac{E}{E_1}\right){/tex} = 0, P {tex}\left(\frac{E}{E_2}\right){/tex} +0, P {tex}\left(\frac{E}{E_3}\right){/tex} = 1, P {tex}\left(\frac{E}{E_4}\right){/tex} = 1 P(E) = P(E 1 ) {tex}\cdot {/tex} P {tex}\left(\frac{E}{E_1}\right){/tex} + P(E 2 ) {tex}\cdot {/tex} P {tex}\left(\frac{E}{E_2}\right){/tex} + P(E 3 ). P {tex}\left(\frac{E}{E_3}\right){/tex} + P(E 4 ) {tex}\cdot {/tex} P {tex}\left(\frac{E}{E_4}\right){/tex} = 0.14 + 0.24 = 0.38 OR By Bayes' Theorem, P(Shell fired from exactly one of them hits the plane) = P[(Shell from A hits the plane and Shell from B does not hit the plane) or (Shell from A does not hit the plane and Shell from B hits the plane)] = 0.3 {tex}\times {/tex} 0.8 + 0.7 {tex}\times {/tex} 0.2 = 0.38 {tex}\frac {\text {P(Shell fired from B hit the plane} \ {\cap} \ \text {Exactly one of them hit the plane)}}{\text{P(Exactly one of them hit the plane)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{P \text { (Shell from only } B \text { hit the plane })}{P \text { (Exactly one of them hit the plane })}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.14}{0.38}=\frac{7}{19}{/tex} P {tex}\left(\frac{E_3}{E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_3\right) \cdot P\left(\frac{E}{E_3}\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(\frac{E}{E_1}\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac{E}{E_2}\right)+P\left(E_3\right)P\left(\frac{E}{E_3}\right)+P\left(E_4\right) \cdot P\left(\frac{E}{E_4}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.14}{0.38}=\frac{7}{19}{/tex} NOTE: The four hypotheses form the partition of the sample space and it can be seen that the sum of their probabilities is 1. question_html:
A bank offers loan to its customers on different types of interest namely, fixed rate, floating rate and variable rate. From the past data with the bank, it is known that a customer avails loan on fixed rate, floating rate or variable rate with probabilities {tex}10 \%, 20 \%{/tex} and {tex}70 \%{/tex} respectively. A customer after availing loan can pay the loan or default on loan repayment. The bank data suggests that the probability that a person defaults on loan after availing it at fixed rate, floating rate and variable rate is {tex}5 \%, 3 \%{/tex} and {tex}1 \%{/tex} respectively.
Based on the above information, answer the following :

  1. What is the probability that a customer after availing the loan will default on the loan repayment? (2)
  2. A customer after availing the loan, defaults on loan repayment. What is the probability that he availed the loan at a variable rate of interest? (2)
question_text: A bank offers loan to its customers on different types of interest namely, fixed rate, floating rate and variable rate. explanation: {tex}E_1{/tex} : customer avails loan on fixed rate {tex}E_2{/tex} : customer avails loan on floating rate {tex}E_3{/tex} : customer avails loan on variable rate A:the person defaults on the loan {tex} {P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{1}{10}, {P}\left(E_2\right)=\frac{2}{10}, {P}\left(E_3\right)=\frac{7}{10} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right) =\frac{5}{100}, {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{3}{100}, {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)=\frac{1}{100} {/tex} {tex}{P}(A) ={P}\left(E_1\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) {/tex} {tex}\cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)+{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right) {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{10} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \times \frac{3}{100}+\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{1}{100} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{18}{1000} \text { or } \frac{9}{500} {/tex} {tex} {P}\left(E_3 \mid A\right) {/tex} {tex}=\frac{{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{{P}\left(E_1\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_2\right)+{P}\left(E_3\right) \cdot {P}\left(A \mid E_3\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{1}{100}}{\frac{18}{1000}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{7}{18} {/tex}

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
To teach the application of probability a maths teacher arranged a surprise game for 5 of his students namely Govind, Girish, Vinod, Abhishek and Ankit. He took a bowl containing tickets numbered 1 to 50 and told the students go one by one and draw two tickets simultaneously from the bowl and replace it after noting the numbers.

  1. Teacher ask Govind, what is the probability that tickets are drawn by Abhishek, shows a prime number on one ticket and a multiple of 4 on other ticket? (1)
  2. Teacher ask Girish, what is the probability that tickets drawn by Ankit, shows an even number on first ticket and an odd number on second ticket? (1)
  3. Teacher asks Abhishek, what is the probability that tickets drawn by Vinod, shows a multiple of 4 on one ticket and a multiple 5 on other ticket? (2)
    OR
    Teacher asks Vinod, what is the probability that both tickets drawn by Girish shows odd number? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: To teach the application of probability a maths teacher arranged a surprise game for 5 of his students namely Govind, Girish, Vinod, Abhishek and Ankit. He took a bowl containing tickets numbered 1 to 50 and told the students go one by one and draw two tickets simultaneously from the bowl and replace it after noting the numbers. Teacher ask Govind, what is the probability that tickets are drawn by Abhishek, shows a prime number on one ticket and a multiple of 4 on other ticket? (1) Teacher ask Girish, what is the probability that tickets drawn by Ankit, shows an even number on first ticket and an odd number on second ticket? (1) Teacher asks Abhishek, what is the probability that tickets drawn by Vinod, shows a multiple of 4 on one ticket and a multiple 5 on other ticket? (2) OR Teacher asks Vinod, what is the probability that both tickets drawn by Girish shows odd number? (2)
explanation: Required probability = P(one ticket with prime number and other ticket with a multiple of 4) {tex}=2\left(\frac{15}{50} \times \frac{12}{49}\right)=\frac{36}{245}{/tex} P(First ticket shows an even number and second ticket shows an odd number) = {tex}\frac{25}{50} \times \frac{25}{49}=\frac{25}{98}{/tex} Required probability = P(one number is a multiple of 4 and other is a multiple of 5) = P(multiple of 5 on first ticket and multiple of 4 on second ticket) + P(multiple of 4 on first ticket and multiple of 5 on second ticket) = {tex}\frac{{10}}{{50}} \times \frac{{12}}{{49}} + \frac{{12}}{{50}} \times \frac{{10}}{{49}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{12}}{{245}} + \frac{{12}}{{245}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{25}}{{245}}{/tex} = {tex} \frac{5}{{49}}{/tex} OR Probability that both tickets drawn by Girish shows odd number = {tex}\frac{{25}}{{50}} \times \frac{{24}}{{49}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{12}}{{49}}{/tex}
solution: Required probability = P(one ticket with prime number and other ticket with a multiple of 4) {tex}=2\left(\frac{15}{50} \times \frac{12}{49}\right)=\frac{36}{245}{/tex} P(First ticket shows an even number and second ticket shows an odd number) = {tex}\frac{25}{50} \times \frac{25}{49}=\frac{25}{98}{/tex} Required probability = P(one number is a multiple of 4 and other is a multiple of 5) = P(multiple of 5 on first ticket and multiple of 4 on second ticket) + P(multiple of 4 on first ticket and multiple of 5 on second ticket) = {tex}\frac{{10}}{{50}} \times \frac{{12}}{{49}} + \frac{{12}}{{50}} \times \frac{{10}}{{49}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{12}}{{245}} + \frac{{12}}{{245}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{25}}{{245}}{/tex} = {tex} \frac{5}{{49}}{/tex} OR Probability that both tickets drawn by Girish shows odd number = {tex}\frac{{25}}{{50}} \times \frac{{24}}{{49}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{12}}{{49}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Required probability = P(one ticket with prime number and other ticket with a multiple of 4)  
    {tex}=2\left(\frac{15}{50} \times \frac{12}{49}\right)=\frac{36}{245}{/tex}
  2. P(First ticket shows an even number and second ticket shows an odd number) 
    = {tex}\frac{25}{50} \times \frac{25}{49}=\frac{25}{98}{/tex}
  3. Required probability = P(one number is a multiple of 4 and other is a multiple of 5)
    = P(multiple of 5 on first ticket and multiple of 4 on second ticket) + P(multiple of 4 on first ticket and multiple of 5 on second ticket)
    = {tex}\frac{{10}}{{50}} \times \frac{{12}}{{49}} + \frac{{12}}{{50}} \times \frac{{10}}{{49}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{{12}}{{245}} + \frac{{12}}{{245}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{{25}}{{245}}{/tex}
    = {tex} \frac{5}{{49}}{/tex}
    OR
    Probability that both tickets drawn by Girish shows odd number
    = {tex}\frac{{25}}{{50}} \times \frac{{24}}{{49}}{/tex} 
    = {tex}\frac{{12}}{{49}}{/tex}

question_html:
Three persons viz. Amber, Bonzi and Comet are manufacturing cars which run on petrol and on battery as well. Their production share in the market is {tex}60 \%, 30 \%{/tex} and {tex}10 \%{/tex} respectively. Of their respective production capacities, {tex}20 \%, 10 \%{/tex} and {tex}5 \%{/tex} cars respectively are electric (or battery operated). Based on the above, answer the following:

  1. (a) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is an electric car? (2)

OR

  1. (b) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is a petrol car? (2)
  2. A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Comet? (1)
  3. A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Amber or Bonzi? (1)
question_text: Three persons viz. Amber, Bonzi and Comet are manufacturing cars which run on petrol and on battery as well. Their production share in the market is {tex}60 \%, 30 \%{/tex} and {tex}10 \%{/tex} respectively. Of their respective production capacities, {tex}20 \%, 10 \%{/tex} and {tex}5 \%{/tex} cars respectively are electric (or battery operated). Based on the above, answer the following: (a) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is an electric car? (2) OR (b) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is a petrol car? (2) A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Comet? (1) A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Amber or Bonzi? (1)
explanation: (i) (a) Let {tex}{A}={Amber}{/tex} manufactures the car {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is electric {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{155}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{31}{200}{/tex} OR (b) Let {tex}{A}={/tex} Amber manufactures the car {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is a petrol car {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{90}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{95}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{845}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{169}{200}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{E}\right)=\frac{P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{P(E)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{50}{10000}}{\frac{1550}{10000}}=\frac{1}{31}{/tex} {tex}{P}\left(\frac{{A} \text { or } {B}}{{E}}\right)=1-{P}\left(\frac{{C}}{{E}}\right)=1-\frac{1}{31}=\frac{30}{31}{/tex}
solution: (i) (a) Let {tex}{A}={Amber}{/tex} manufactures the car {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is electric {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{155}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{31}{200}{/tex} OR (b) Let {tex}{A}={/tex} Amber manufactures the car {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is a petrol car {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{90}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{95}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{845}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{169}{200}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{E}\right)=\frac{P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{P(E)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{50}{10000}}{\frac{1550}{10000}}=\frac{1}{31}{/tex} {tex}{P}\left(\frac{{A} \text { or } {B}}{{E}}\right)=1-{P}\left(\frac{{C}}{{E}}\right)=1-\frac{1}{31}=\frac{30}{31}{/tex}

1. (i) (a) Let {tex}{A}={Amber}{/tex} manufactures the car

    {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}{/tex}

    {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{90}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{95}{100}{/tex}

    {tex}=\frac{\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\frac{50}{10000}}{\frac{1550}{10000}}=\frac{1}{31}{/tex}

question_html:
A gardener wanted to plant vegetables in his garden. Hence he bought 10 seeds of brinjal plant, 12 seeds of cabbage plant and 8 seeds of radish plant. The shopkeeper assured him of germination probabilities of brinjal, cabbage and radish to be {tex}25 \%, 35 \%{/tex} and {tex}40 \%{/tex} respectively. But before he could plant the seeds, they got mixed up in the bag and he had to sow them randomly.

Based upon the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Calculate the probability of a randomly chosen seed to germinate. (2)
  2. What is the probability that it is a cabbage seed, given that the chosen seed germinates? (2)
question_text: A gardener wanted to plant vegetables in his garden. Hence he bought 10 seeds of brinjal plant, 12 seeds of cabbage plant and 8 seeds of radish plant. The shopkeeper assured him of germination probabilities of brinjal, cabbage and radish to be {tex}25 \%, 35 \%{/tex} and {tex}40 \%{/tex} respectively. But before he could plant the seeds, they got mixed up in the bag and he had to sow them randomly. Based upon the above information, answer the following questions: Calculate the probability of a randomly chosen seed to germinate. (2) What is the probability that it is a cabbage seed, given that the chosen seed germinates? (2)
explanation: Let A: Event that chosen seed germinates. B: Event that Brinjal seed is chosen. C: Event that Cabbage seed is chosen. R: Event that Radish seed is chosen. {tex}P(B)=\frac{10}{30} ; P(C)=\frac{12}{30} ; P(R)=\frac{8}{30} ;{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)=\frac{25}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)=\frac{35}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right)=\frac{40}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(A) =P(B) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}+P(R) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{30} \times \frac{25}{100}+\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}+\frac{8}{30} \times \frac{40}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{990}{3000} \text { or } \frac{33}{100}{/tex} (a) {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{A}\right)=\frac{P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)}{P(B) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)+P(R) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right)} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}}{\frac{990}{3000}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{42}{99} \text { or } \frac{14}{33}{/tex}
solution: Let A: Event that chosen seed germinates. B: Event that Brinjal seed is chosen. C: Event that Cabbage seed is chosen. R: Event that Radish seed is chosen. {tex}P(B)=\frac{10}{30} ; P(C)=\frac{12}{30} ; P(R)=\frac{8}{30} ;{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)=\frac{25}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)=\frac{35}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right)=\frac{40}{100}{/tex} {tex}P(A) =P(B) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}+P(R) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{10}{30} \times \frac{25}{100}+\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}+\frac{8}{30} \times \frac{40}{100}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{990}{3000} \text { or } \frac{33}{100}{/tex} (a) {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{A}\right)=\frac{P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)}{P(B) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)+P(R) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right)} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}}{\frac{990}{3000}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{42}{99} \text { or } \frac{14}{33}{/tex}

Let A: Event that chosen seed germinates.
B: Event that Brinjal seed is chosen.
C: Event that Cabbage seed is chosen.
R: Event that Radish seed is chosen.
{tex}P(B)=\frac{10}{30} ; P(C)=\frac{12}{30} ; P(R)=\frac{8}{30} ;{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)=\frac{25}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right)=\frac{35}{100} ; P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right)=\frac{40}{100}{/tex}

  1. {tex}P(A) =P(B) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{B}\right)+P(C) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{C}\right){/tex} {tex}+P(R) \cdot P\left(\frac{A}{R}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{10}{30} \times \frac{25}{100}+\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}+\frac{8}{30} \times \frac{40}{100}{/tex}

    {tex}=\frac{\frac{12}{30} \times \frac{35}{100}}{\frac{990}{3000}}{/tex}

question_html:
Two persons are competing for a position on the Managing Committee of an organisation. The probabilities that the first and the second person will be appointed are 0.5 and 0.6 respectively. Also, if the first person gets appointed, then the probability of introducing waste treatment plant is {tex}0. 7{/tex} and the corresponding probability is {tex}0.4{/tex}, if the second person gets appointed.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. What is the probability that the waste treatment plant is introduced?
  2. After the selection, if the waste treatment plant is introduced, what is the probability that the first person had introduced it?
question_text: Two persons are competing for a position on the Managing Committee of an organisation. The probabilities that the first and the second person will be appointed are 0.5 and 0.6 respectively. Also, if the first person gets appointed, then the probability of introducing waste treatment plant is {tex}0. 7{/tex} and the corresponding probability is {tex}0.4{/tex} , if the second person gets appointed. Based on the above information, answer the following questions: What is the probability that the waste treatment plant is introduced? After the selection, if the waste treatment plant is introduced, what is the probability that the first person had introduced it?
explanation: {tex}E_{1}{/tex} : Event that the first person is appointed. {tex}E_{2}{/tex} : Event that the second person is appointed. A: Event that the waste treatment plant is introducted. Here, {tex}{P}\left(E_{1}\right)=0.5, P\left(E_{2}\right)=0.6{/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right)=0.7, P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right)=0.4{/tex} {tex}P{/tex} (waste treatment plant is introducted) {tex}=P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A \mid E_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=(0.5)(0.7)+(0.6)(0.4){/tex} {tex}=0.35+0.24=0.59{/tex} {tex} P\left(E_1 \mid A\right) =\frac{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{(0.5)(0.7)}{(0.5)(0.7)+(0.6)(0.4)} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{0.35}{0.59}=\frac{35}{59}{/tex}

question_html:
A biased die is tossed and respective probabilities for various faces to turn up are the following:

Face 1 2 3 4 5 6
Probability 0·1 0·24 0·19 0·18 0·15 K

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. What is the value of K? (2)
  2. If a face showing an even number has turned up, then what is the probability that it is the face with 2 or 4? (2)
question_text: A biased die is tossed and respective probabilities for various faces to turn up are the following: Face 1 2 3 4 5 6 Probability 0·1 0·24 0·19 0·18 0·15 K Based on the above information, answer the following questions: What is the value of K? (2) If a face showing an even number has turned up, then what is the probability that it is the face with 2 or 4? (2)
explanation: P(1) + P(2) + P(3) + P(4) + P(5) + P(6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.1 + 0.24 + 0.19 + 0.18 + 0.15 + K = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K = 0.14 A: face shows 2 or 4 B: even face have turned up Required probability = P (A/B) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})}{/tex} here P (A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(2) + P(4) = 0.24 + 0.18 = 0.42 P(B) = P(2) + P(4) + P(6) = 0.56 {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{0.42}{0.56}{/tex} = {tex}\frac34{/tex}
solution: P(1) + P(2) + P(3) + P(4) + P(5) + P(6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.1 + 0.24 + 0.19 + 0.18 + 0.15 + K = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K = 0.14 A: face shows 2 or 4 B: even face have turned up Required probability = P (A/B) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})}{/tex} here P (A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(2) + P(4) = 0.24 + 0.18 = 0.42 P(B) = P(2) + P(4) + P(6) = 0.56 {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{0.42}{0.56}{/tex} = {tex}\frac34{/tex}
solution_html:
1. P(1) + P(2) + P(3) + P(4) + P(5) + P(6)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.1 + 0.24 + 0.19 + 0.18 + 0.15 + K = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K = 0.14

  2. A: face shows 2 or 4
    B: even face have turned up
    Required probability = P (A/B) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B})}{\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})}{/tex}
    here P (A{tex}\cap{/tex}B) = P(2) + P(4)
    = 0.24 + 0.18 = 0.42
    P(B) = P(2) + P(4) + P(6) = 0.56
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(A/B) = {tex}\frac{0.42}{0.56}{/tex} = {tex}\frac34{/tex}

question_html:
Some students are having a misconception while comparing decimals. For example, a student may mention that {tex}78.56 \gt 78.9{/tex} as {tex}7856 \gt 789{/tex}. In order to assess this concept, a decimal comparison test was administered to the students of class VI through the following question : In the recently held Sports Day in the school, 5 students participated in a javelin throw competition. The distances to which they have thrown the javelin are shown below in the table :

Name of student Distance of javelin (in meters)
Ajay {tex}47 .7{/tex}
Bijoy {tex}47 .07{/tex}
Kartik {tex}43.09{/tex}
Dinesh {tex}43 . 9{/tex}
Devesh {tex}45.2{/tex}

The students were asked to identify who has thrown the javelin the farthest.
Based on the test attempted by the students, the teacher concludes that {tex}40 \%{/tex} of the students have the misconception in the concept of decimal comparison and the rest do not have the misconception. {tex}80 \%{/tex} of the students having misconception answered Bijoy as the correct answer in the paper. {tex}90 \%{/tex} of the students who are identified with not having misconception, did not answer Bijoy as their answer.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions :

  1. What is the probability of a student not having misconception but still answers Bijoy in the test? (1)
  2. What is the probability that a randomly selected student answers Bijoy as his answer in the test? (1)
    1. What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is having misconception? (2)

      OR

    2.  What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is amongst students who do not have the misconception? (2)
question_text: Some students are having a misconception while comparing decimals. For example, a student may mention that {tex}78.56 \gt 78.9{/tex} as {tex}7856 \gt 789{/tex} . In order to assess this concept, a decimal comparison test was administered to the students of class VI through the following question : In the recently held Sports Day in the school, 5 students participated in a javelin throw competition. The distances to which they have thrown the javelin are shown below in the table : Name of student Distance of javelin (in meters) Ajay {tex}47 .7{/tex} Bijoy {tex}47 .07{/tex} Kartik {tex}43.09{/tex} Dinesh {tex}43 . 9{/tex} Devesh {tex}45.2{/tex} The students were asked to identify who has thrown the javelin the farthest. Based on the test attempted by the students, the teacher concludes that {tex}40 \%{/tex} of the students have the misconception in the concept of decimal comparison and the rest do not have the misconception. {tex}80 \%{/tex} of the students having misconception answered Bijoy as the correct answer in the paper. {tex}90 \%{/tex} of the students who are identified with not having misconception, did not answer Bijoy as their answer. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions : What is the probability of a student not having misconception but still answers Bijoy in the test? (1) What is the probability that a randomly selected student answers Bijoy as his answer in the test? (1) What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is having misconception? (2) OR What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is amongst students who do not have the misconception? (2)
explanation: Let {tex}E_1{/tex} : Student has a misconception {tex}E_2{/tex} : Student does not have misconception A: Student answered Bijoy as correct {tex} \therefore P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{40}{100}, {P}\left(E_2\right)=\frac{60}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(A \mid E_1\right)=\frac{80}{100}, P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_1\right)=\frac{20}{100}, P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_2\right)=\frac{90}{100} {/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}P(A)={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right){/tex} {tex} =\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{38}{100} \text { or } \frac{19}{50} {/tex} {tex}P\left(E_1 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)}{P(A)}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{16}{19} {/tex} OR {tex}P\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{3}{19} {/tex}
solution: Let {tex}E_1{/tex} : Student has a misconception {tex}E_2{/tex} : Student does not have misconception A: Student answered Bijoy as correct {tex} \therefore P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{40}{100}, {P}\left(E_2\right)=\frac{60}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(A \mid E_1\right)=\frac{80}{100}, P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100} {/tex} {tex} P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_1\right)=\frac{20}{100}, P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_2\right)=\frac{90}{100} {/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}P(A)={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right){/tex} {tex} =\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{38}{100} \text { or } \frac{19}{50} {/tex} {tex}P\left(E_1 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)}{P(A)}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{16}{19} {/tex} OR {tex}P\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{3}{19} {/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}E_1{/tex} : Student has a misconception
{tex}E_2{/tex} : Student does not have misconception
A: Student answered Bijoy as correct
{tex} \therefore P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{40}{100}, {P}\left(E_2\right)=\frac{60}{100} {/tex}
{tex} P\left(A \mid E_1\right)=\frac{80}{100}, P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100} {/tex}
{tex} P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_1\right)=\frac{20}{100}, P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_2\right)=\frac{90}{100} {/tex}

  1. {tex}P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
  2. {tex}P(A)={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right){/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{38}{100} \text { or } \frac{19}{50} {/tex}
    1. {tex}P\left(E_1 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)}{P(A)}{/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{16}{19} {/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}P\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)}{/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{3}{19} {/tex}

question_html:
A shopkeeper sells three types of flower seeds A1, A2, A3. They are sold in the form of a mixture, where the proportions of these seeds are 4 : 4 : 2, respectively. The germination rates of the three types of seeds are 45%, 60% and 35% respectively.

  1. Calculate the probability that a randomly chosen seed will germinate. (2)
  2. Calculate the probability that the seed is of type A2, given that a randomly chosen seed germinates. (2)
question_text: A shopkeeper sells three types of flower seeds A1, A2, A3. They are sold in the form of a mixture, where the proportions of these seeds are 4 : 4 : 2, respectively. The germination rates of the three types of seeds are 45%, 60% and 35% respectively. Calculate the probability that a randomly chosen seed will germinate. (2) Calculate the probability that the seed is of type A2, given that a randomly chosen seed germinates. (2)
explanation: We have, A 1 : A 2 : A 4 = 4 : 4 : 2 P(A1) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex} , P(A 2 ) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex} and P(A 3 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{10}{/tex} where A 1 , A 2 and A 3 denote the three types of flower seed. Let E be the event that a seed germinates and {tex}\bar {E}{/tex} be the event that a seed does not germinate. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {45} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {60} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {35} {100}{/tex} And {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {55} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {40} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {65} {100}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E) = P(A 1 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} + P(A 2 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} + P(A 3 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{1000}=\frac{490}{1000}{/tex} = 0.49 {tex}P (\frac {A_2} {E}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})}{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_1\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_1}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_3\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_3}})}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{10} \times \frac{60}{100}}{\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{490}{1000}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {24} {49}{/tex} = 0.48
solution: We have, A 1 : A 2 : A 4 = 4 : 4 : 2 P(A1) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex} , P(A 2 ) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex} and P(A 3 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{10}{/tex} where A 1 , A 2 and A 3 denote the three types of flower seed. Let E be the event that a seed germinates and {tex}\bar {E}{/tex} be the event that a seed does not germinate. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {45} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {60} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {35} {100}{/tex} And {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {55} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {40} {100}{/tex} , {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {65} {100}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E) = P(A 1 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} + P(A 2 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} + P(A 3 ) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{1000}=\frac{490}{1000}{/tex} = 0.49 {tex}P (\frac {A_2} {E}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})}{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_1\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_1}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_3\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_3}})}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{10} \times \frac{60}{100}}{\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{490}{1000}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {24} {49}{/tex} = 0.48
solution_html:
1. We have, A1 : A2 : A4 = 4 : 4 : 2
    P(A1) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex}, P(A2) = {tex}\frac{4}{10}{/tex} and P(A3) = {tex}\frac{2}{10}{/tex}
    where A1, A2 and A3 denote the three types of flower seed.
    Let E be the event that a seed germinates and {tex}\bar {E}{/tex} be the event that a seed does not germinate.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {45} {100}{/tex}, {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {60} {100}{/tex}, {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {35} {100}{/tex}
    And {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_1}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {55} {100}{/tex}, {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {40} {100}{/tex}, {tex}P (\frac {\bar E} {A_3}){/tex} = {tex}\frac {65} {100}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E) = P(A1) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_1}){/tex} + P(A2) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_2}){/tex} + P(A3) {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex}P (\frac {E} {A_3}){/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{1000}=\frac{490}{1000}{/tex} = 0.49

  2. {tex}P (\frac {A_2} {E}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})}{\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_1\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_1}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_2\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_2}})+\mathrm P\left({\mathrm A}_3\right)\cdot\mathrm P({\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm E}{{\mathrm A}_3}})}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{10} \times \frac{60}{100}}{\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{45}{100}+\frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{60}{100}+\frac{2}{10} \cdot \frac{35}{100}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{180}{1000}+\frac{240}{1000}+\frac{70}{100}}{/tex} 
    = {tex}\frac{\frac{240}{1000}}{\frac{490}{1000}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {24} {49}{/tex} = 0.48

  3. What is the probability that he answers correctly given that he had made a guess? (1)
    1. {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

    3. 1

  5. {tex}\sum_\limits {\mathrm{k}=1}^3 \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{k}} \mid \mathrm{A}\right){/tex} is: (1)

    1. 0

    2. {tex}\frac 13{/tex}

    3. 1

The probability that he makes a guess is 13 and the probability that he copies the answer is 16. The probability that his answer is correct given that he copied is 18. question_html: A doctor is to visit a patient. The probabilities that he will be late are {tex}\frac{1}{4},\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{{12}}{/tex}, if he comes by train, bus and scooter respectively, but if he comes by other means of transport, that he will not be late. When he arrives, he is late. What is the probability that he comes by train?
question_text: A doctor is to visit a patient. From the past experience, it is known that the probabilities that he will come by train, bus, scooter or by other means of transport are respectively {tex}\frac{3}{{10}},\frac{1}{5},\frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} . The probabilities that he will be late are {tex}\frac{1}{4},\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{{12}}{/tex} , if he comes by train, bus and scooter respectively, but if he comes by other means of transport, that he will not be late. When he arrives, he is late. What is the probability that he comes by train?
explanation: Let E be the event that the doctor visits the patient late and let T 1 , T 2 , T 3 , T 4 , be the event that the doctor comes by train, bus, scooter and other means of Transport respectively. P(T 1 ) = {tex} \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex} , P(T 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} , P(T 3 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} , P(T 4 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} P( {tex}\frac {E}{T_1}{/tex} ) = probability that the doctor arriving late comes by train {tex}= \frac{1}{4}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}P(\frac {E}{T_2}) = \frac{1}{3},P(\frac {E}{T_3}) = \frac{1}{{12}},P(\frac {E}{T_4}) = 0{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {T_1}{E}) {/tex} = {tex} \frac{{P({T_1})P(E/{T_1})}}{{P({T_1})P(E/{T_1}) + P({T_2})P(E/{T_2}) + P({T_3})P(E/{T_3}) + P({T_4})P(E/{T_4})}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{3}{10}\times \frac{1}{4}}{\frac{3}{10}\times\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{5}\times\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{10}\times\frac{1}{12}+\frac{2}{5}\times 0}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

Let E be the event that the doctor visits the patient late and let T1, T2, T3, T4, be the event that the doctor comes by train, bus, scooter and other means of Transport respectively.

question_text: A laboratory blood test is 99% effective in detecting a certain disease when it is in fact, present. However, the test also yields a false positive result for 0.5% of the healthy person tested (i. e if a healthy person is tested, then, with probability 0.005, the test will imply he has the disease). If 0.1 percent of the population actually has the disease, what is the probability that a person has the disease given that his test result is positive?
explanation: E 1 : the person has the disease E 2 : the person is healthy A : test is positive P(E 1 ) = 0.1 = {tex} \frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}1 - \frac{1}{{10}} = \frac{9}{{10}}{/tex} P( {tex}\frac {A}{E_1}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{{99}}{{100}}{/tex} , {tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right) {/tex} = 0.005 {tex} = \frac{5}{{1000}}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{A}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{{99}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{10}}}}{{\frac{{99}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{10}} + \frac{5}{{1000}} + \frac{9}{{10}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{22}}{{23}}{/tex}

question_html:
Suppose that the reliability of a HIV test is specified as follows:
Of people having HIV, 90% of the test detect the disease but 10% go undetected. Of people free of HIV, 99% of the test are Judged HIV-ive but 1% are diagnosed as showing HIV+ive. From a large population of which only 0.1% have HIV, one person is selected at random, given the HIV test, and the pathologist reports him/her is HIV+ive. What is the probability that the person actually has HIV?
question_text: Suppose that the reliability of a HIV test is specified as follows: Of people having HIV, 90% of the test detect the disease but 10% go undetected. Of people free of HIV, 99% of the test are Judged HIV-ive but 1% are diagnosed as showing HIV+ive. From a large population of which only 0.1% have HIV, one person is selected at random, given the HIV test, and the pathologist reports him/her is HIV+ive. What is the probability that the person actually has HIV?
explanation: Let E denote the event that the person selected is actually having HIV and A the event that the person’s HIV test is diagnosed as +ve. Let E' denote the event that person selected is actually not having HIV. Clearly, {E, E'} is a partition of the sample space of all people in the population. We are given that P(E) = 0.1% = {tex}\frac{{0.1}}{{100}} {/tex} = 0.001 P(E') = 1 - P(E) = 0.999 {tex}P(\frac {A}{E}) {/tex} = 90% = {tex} \frac{{90}}{{100}} {/tex} = 0.9 {tex}P(\frac {A}{E'}) {/tex} = 1% = {tex} \frac{1}{{100}}{/tex} = 0.01 By Bayes theorm,we have, {tex}P(\frac {E}{A}) = \frac{{P(E)P(A/E)}}{{P(E)P(A/E) + P(E')P(A/E')}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.001 \times 0.9}}{{0.001 \times 0.9 + 0.999 \times 0.01}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{9}{{9 + 99.9}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{90}}{{1089}}{/tex} = 0.083

We are given that P(E) = 0.1% = {tex}\frac{{0.1}}{{100}} {/tex} = 0.001 P(E') = 1 - P(E) = 0.999 {tex}P(\frac {A}{E}) {/tex} = 90% = {tex} \frac{{90}}{{100}} {/tex} = 0.9 {tex}P(\frac {A}{E'}) {/tex} = 1% = {tex} \frac{1}{{100}}{/tex} = 0.01 By Bayes theorm,we have, {tex}P(\frac {E}{A}) = \frac{{P(E)P(A/E)}}{{P(E)P(A/E) + P(E')P(A/E')}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.001 \times 0.9}}{{0.001 \times 0.9 + 0.999 \times 0.01}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{9}{{9 + 99.9}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{90}}{{1089}}{/tex} = 0.083
solution_html:
Let E denote the event that the person selected is actually having HIV and A the event that the person’s HIV test is diagnosed as +ve.
Let E' denote the event that person selected is actually not having HIV.
Clearly, {E, E'} is a partition of the sample space of all people in the population.
We are given that
P(E) = 0.1% = {tex}\frac{{0.1}}{{100}} {/tex} = 0.001

question_html: Bag I contains 4 red and 2 green balls and Bag II contains 3 red and 5 green balls. question_text: Bag I contains 4 red and 2 green balls and Bag II contains 3 red and 5 green balls. One ball is transferred at random from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn at random from Bag II. The ball so drawn is found to be green in colour. Find the probability that the transferred ball is also green.
explanation: Let E 1 : Transferred ball is green E 2 : Transferred ball is red A: Green ball is found Here, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{6}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{4}{6}{/tex} P(A/E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{6}{9}{/tex} , P(A/E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex} Using Baye’s theorem P(E 1 /A) = {tex}\frac{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}}{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}+\frac{4}{6} \times \frac{5}{9}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{12}{12+20}=\frac{3}{8}{/tex}
solution: Let E 1 : Transferred ball is green E 2 : Transferred ball is red A: Green ball is found Here, P(E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{2}{6}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{4}{6}{/tex} P(A/E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{6}{9}{/tex} , P(A/E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex} Using Baye’s theorem P(E 1 /A) = {tex}\frac{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}}{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}+\frac{4}{6} \times \frac{5}{9}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{12}{12+20}=\frac{3}{8}{/tex}

Let E1: Transferred ball is green
E2: Transferred ball is red

P(A/E1) = {tex}\frac{6}{9}{/tex}, P(A/E2) = {tex}\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
Using Baye’s theorem
P(E1/A) = {tex}\frac{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} / {E}_{2}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}}{\frac{2}{6} \times \frac{6}{9}+\frac{4}{6} \times \frac{5}{9}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{12}{12+20}=\frac{3}{8}{/tex}

solution_html:
Let us define the following events:
E1 = one red and one black ball is transferred 
E2 = two red balls are transferred
E3 = two black balls are transferred
E = drawn ball is red.
Then,
P(E) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 3 } C _ { 1 } \times ^ { 5 } C _ { 1 } } { 8 } = \frac { 3 \times 5 } { 28 } = \frac { 15 } { 28 }{/tex}
P(E2) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 3 } C _ { 2 } } {^{ 8} { C _ { 2 } } } = \frac { 3 } { 28 }{/tex}
P(E3) = {tex} \frac { ^ { 5 } C _ { 2 } } { ^ { 8 } C _ { 2 } } = \frac { 10 } { 28 }{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac {E}{E_1}) = \frac {5}{10}{/tex}
{tex}P (\frac {E}{ E _ { 2 }}) = \frac { 6 } { 10 }{/tex}
{tex} P(\frac {E}{{E_3}}) = \frac{4}{{10}}{/tex}
Now, required probability= P({tex}\frac {E_2}{E}{/tex})
{tex} = \frac { P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 2 } \right) } { P \left( E _ { 1 } \right) P \left( E / E _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 2 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 2 } \right) + P \left( E _ { 3 } \right) \cdot P \left( E / E _ { 3 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \frac { 3 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 6 } { 10 } } { \frac { 15 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 5 } { 10 } + \frac { 3 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 6 } { 10 } + \frac { 10 } { 28 } \cdot \frac { 4 } { 10 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 18 } { 75 + 18 + 40 } = \frac { 18 } { 133 }{/tex}
